sections.heading: Money laundering is the manipulation of money or property in order to disguise its
sections.title: Definition of money laundering
sections.summary: This section defines money laundering as the manipulation of money or property to disguise its true source. It explains that criminal activities generate illegal earnings that cannot be explained, and using such funds risks exposing criminals to prosecution and forfeiture of illicit proceeds. The definition emphasizes the criminal intent behind the manipulation of funds.

requirements.text: Money laundering is the manipulation of money or property in order to disguise its true source

sections.source_quote: Money laundering is the manipulation of money or property in order to disguise its true source.

sections.heading: In order to benefit from the proceeds of unlawful activity, criminals must conceal
sections.title: Concealment of funds origins in money laundering
sections.summary: This section states that criminals must conceal the origins of funds obtained from unlawful activities to benefit from the proceeds. It explains that successful money laundering results in illicit proceeds appearing legitimate and no longer associated with the underlying criminal activity. The process aims to obscure the source of funds to avoid detection by authorities.

requirements.text: Criminals must conceal the origins of funds obtained from unlawful activities

sections.source_quote: In order to benefit from the proceeds of unlawful activity, criminals must conceal the origins of these funds.

sections.heading: The financing of terrorism involves the solicitation, collection and the providing of
sections.title: Definition of terrorist financing
sections.summary: This section defines terrorist financing as the solicitation, collection, and provision of funds and assets with the intention of supporting terrorist acts, organizations, or individuals. It clarifies that these funds may originate from legal or illicit sources, and the primary goal is to conceal both the financing and the nature of the activity being financed, unlike money laundering which focuses on concealing the source of funds.

requirements.text: Terrorist financing involves the solicitation, collection and providing of funds and other assets with the intention that it may be used to support terrorist acts, terrorist organisations or individual terrorists

sections.source_quote: The financing of terrorism involves the solicitation, collection and the providing of funds and other assets with the intention that it may be used to support terrorist acts, terrorist organisations or individual terrorists.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure definition of terrorist financing, not data compliance

sections.heading: According to international best practice risk rating methodology, risk refers to the
sections.title: Definition of risk in risk rating
sections.summary: This section defines risk in the context of international best practice risk rating methodology as the likelihood and impact of uncertain events on set objectives. It specifies that impact can be positive or negative deviations from expectations and that uncertainty arises from three factors: threat, vulnerability, and consequence.

requirements.text: Risk refers to the likelihood and impact of uncertain events on set objectives

sections.source_quote: According to international best practice risk rating methodology, risk refers to the likelihood and impact of uncertain events on set objectives.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines risk in risk rating methodology

sections.heading: The context of the above is important.  For example, "threat" or "consequence" to
sections.title: Context of risk factors
sections.summary: This section explains that the context of risk factors is important, particularly for 'threat' and 'consequence'—which must be defined in terms of who or what they affect. It also notes that vulnerability can arise from external and internal factors and may be either controllable or uncontrollable.

requirements.text: The context of 'threat' or 'consequence' is important to define who or what they affect
Vulnerability can arise from external and internal factors and may be either controllable or uncontrollable

sections.source_quote: The context of the above is important. For example, "threat" or "consequence" to whom or what.

sections.heading: A threat is a person or group of people, object or activity with the potential to cause
sections.title: Definition of threat in risk context
sections.summary: This section defines a threat as a person, group of people, object, or activity with the potential to cause harm. In the context of money laundering and terrorist financing, threats include criminals, terrorist groups, their facilitators, funds, and past, present, and future money laundering or terrorist financing activities.

requirements.text: A threat is a person or group of people, object or activity with the potential to cause harm

In the context of money laundering and terrorist financing, threats include criminals, terrorist groups and their facilitators, funds, and past, present and future money laundering or terrorist financing activities

sections.heading: The concept of vulnerabilities comprises those things that can be exploited by the
sections.title: Definition of vulnerabilities in security context
sections.summary: This section defines vulnerabilities as elements that can be exploited by threats or facilitate their activities. It distinguishes vulnerabilities from threats by focusing on weaknesses or features in systems, institutions, products, or services that could be exploited. The text specifies that vulnerabilities are distinct from threats and may arise in various areas, though detailed discussions are deferred to later sections of the guidance.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines vulnerabilities as exploitable weaknesses in systems that could be leveraged by threats, relevant for assessing security risks in data transfers.

requirements.text: Vulnerabilities are things that can be exploited by threats or support their activities
Vulnerabilities focus on weaknesses or features that may be exploited in systems, institutions, products, or services

sections.source_quote: The concept of vulnerabilities comprises those things that can be exploited by the threat or that may support or facilitate its activities.

sections.heading: Risk in the context of money laundering or terrorist financing can therefore be
sections.title: Risk definition for money laundering and terrorist financing
sections.summary: This section defines risk in the context of money laundering or terrorist financing as the likelihood and impact of such activities that could materialize due to threats and vulnerabilities within an accountable institution. It emphasizes that risk arises from the combination of threats and vulnerabilities that manifest in financial institutions, affecting their ability to detect and prevent illicit activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines risk as the likelihood and impact of money laundering or terrorist financing activities, critical for assessing the security implications of data transfers.

requirements.text: Risk in the context of money laundering or terrorist financing is the likelihood and impact of such activities

sections.source_quote: Risk in the context of money laundering or terrorist financing can therefore be thought of as the likelihood and impact of money laundering or terrorist financing activities that could materialise as a result of a combination of threats and vulnerabilities manifesting in an accountable institution.

sections.heading: Residual risk is the level of risk that remains after controls and mitigation measures
sections.title: Definition of residual risk after controls
sections.summary: This section defines residual risk as the level of risk remaining after an accountable institution implements controls and mitigation measures. It specifies that residual risk is the outcome of applying these measures to address vulnerabilities and threats within the institution's systems and operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Residual risk is the remaining risk after security controls are applied, relevant for assessing the effectiveness of data transfer safeguards.

requirements.text: Residual risk is the level of risk remaining after controls and mitigation measures were implemented by the accountable institution

sections.source_quote: Residual risk is the level of risk that remains after controls and mitigation measures were implemented by the accountable institution.

sections.heading: The FIC Act requires accountable institutions to apply a risk-based approach when
sections.title: Risk-based approach for customer due diligence
sections.summary: This section mandates that accountable institutions must apply a risk-based approach when conducting customer due diligence measures under the FIC Act. It specifies that this approach is required to manage money laundering and terrorist financing risks effectively within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to use risk-based customer due diligence, critical for assessing the security of data transfers involving financial entities.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must apply a risk-based approach when carrying out customer due diligence measures

sections.source_quote: The FIC Act requires accountable institutions to apply a risk-based approach when carrying out customer due diligence measures.

sections.heading: The concept of ML/TF risks, as the term implies, relate to threats and vulnerabilities
sections.title: ML/TF risks relate to threats and vulnerabilities
sections.summary: This section defines money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks as threats and vulnerabilities that could promote illicit financial activities or jeopardize the detection, investigation, or prosecution of such activities. It emphasizes that ML/TF risks impact both the ability to detect illicit activities and the legal consequences for offenders.
sections.dtia_summary: ML/TF risks are threats and vulnerabilities that could facilitate illicit activities or hinder detection, relevant for assessing data transfer security.

requirements.text: ML/TF risks relate to threats and vulnerabilities that may promote laundering of proceeds of unlawful activities or financing of terrorism
ML/TF risks may jeopardize detection, investigation, or prosecution of illicit activities

sections.source_quote: The concept of ML/TF risks, as the term implies, relate to threats and vulnerabilities that may promote the laundering of proceeds of unlawful activities or the financing of terrorism, on the one hand, or may jeopardise the detection, investigation or prosecution of these activities or the possibility of the forfeiture of proceeds of unlawful activities, on the other.

sections.heading: On a national level these are threats and vulnerabilities which put at risk the
sections.title: National-level threats and vulnerabilities
sections.summary: This section describes national-level threats and vulnerabilities that risk the integrity of South Africa's financial system and negatively impact criminal justice administration, thereby affecting the safety and security of South Africans and people outside the country. It highlights the broader implications of these risks on national security and international stability.
sections.dtia_summary: National threats and vulnerabilities risk financial system integrity and criminal justice, relevant for assessing cross-border data transfer security.

requirements.text: National threats and vulnerabilities put at risk the integrity of South Africa's financial system
National threats and vulnerabilities negatively impact the administration of criminal justice

sections.source_quote: On a national level these are threats and vulnerabilities which put at risk the integrity of South Africa's financial system and negatively impacts the administration of criminal justice which affects the safety and security of South Africans as well as that of people outside of South Africa.

sections.heading: In relation to accountable institutions, ML/TF risks are threats and vulnerabilities
sections.title: ML/TF risks as threats to accountable institutions
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must recognize that money laundering and terrorist financing risks pose threats and vulnerabilities that could be exploited to facilitate such activities. These risks arise from clients using the institution's products and services, and relate to client markets, geographic locations, delivery channels, and product features. The risks impact operational, line management, and strategic objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines ML/TF risks as threats that could be exploited for money laundering and terrorist financing, requiring institutions to assess how these risks affect their operations and strategic objectives during data transfers.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must recognize ML/TF risks as threats and vulnerabilities that could be exploited to facilitate money laundering or terrorist financing activities
ML/TF risks relate to clients using the institution's products and services to promote money laundering or terrorist financing

sections.source_quote: In relation to accountable institutions, ML/TF risks are threats and vulnerabilities which put the accountable institution at risk of being abused in order to facilitate ML/TF activities.

sections.heading: In order to have a robust ML/TF risk management system accountable institutions
sections.title: Robust ML/TF risk management system requirements
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must demonstrate how they contextualize ML/TF risks within their specific business operations to impact their operational, line management, and strategic objectives. This requires institutions to integrate risk assessment into their business practices and decision-making processes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that institutions must contextualize ML/TF risks within their business operations to ensure they impact operational and strategic decisions, which is critical for data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must demonstrate how they contextualize ML/TF risks within their particular businesses as having an impact on operational, line management and strategic objectives

sections.source_quote: In order to have a robust ML/TF risk management system accountable institutions must be able to demonstrate how they contextualise the concepts of "ML/TF risk" within their particular businesses as having an impact on their operational, line management and strategic objectives.

sections.heading: Controls should be purposefully built and/or adapted to address ML/TF risks.
sections.title: Purposeful controls for ML/TF risk mitigation
sections.summary: Accountable institutions should implement controls specifically designed to address ML/TF risks. Institutions may utilize existing controls without needing to create new ones, emphasizing the importance of practical and adaptive risk management measures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to build or adapt controls purposefully to address ML/TF risks, which is relevant for data transfers as it impacts how institutions manage risks associated with cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Controls should be purposefully built and/or adapted to address ML/TF risks
Accountable institutions may make use of existing controls

sections.source_quote: Controls should be purposefully built and/or adapted to address ML/TF risks. Accountable institutions may make use of the controls which are already in place.

sections.heading: ML/TF risk management is a process that includes the identification of ML/TF risks,
sections.title: ML/TF risk management process framework
sections.summary: ML/TF risk management involves identifying ML/TF risks, assessing their severity, and developing methods to manage and mitigate identified risks. This process is essential for institutions to proactively address vulnerabilities in their operations and client interactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the ML/TF risk management process, which is critical for data transfers as it requires institutions to systematically identify and mitigate risks associated with cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: ML/TF risk management includes identification of ML/TF risks
ML/TF risk management includes assessment of identified ML/TF risks
ML/TF risk management includes development of methods to manage and mitigate identified ML/TF risks

sections.source_quote: ML/TF risk management is a process that includes the identification of ML/TF risks, the assessment of these risks, and the development of methods to manage and mitigate the risks that have been identified.

sections.heading: According to ISO 31000: 2009 (International Organization for Standardization), risk
sections.title: ML/TF risk management strategies per ISO 31000
sections.summary: ML/TF risks can be managed through strategies such as avoiding the risk, accepting or increasing the risk, removing the risk source, changing likelihood or consequences, sharing the risk, or retaining the risk. These strategies must be applied within the context of the institution's risk appetite and business operations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides specific strategies for managing ML/TF risks in line with ISO 31000 standards, which are critical for data transfers as they define how institutions should mitigate risks associated with cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: ML/TF risks may be managed by avoiding the risk through not starting or continuing activities that give rise to the risk
ML/TF risks may be managed by accepting or increasing the risk to pursue opportunities
ML/TF risks may be managed by removing the risk source
ML/TF risks may be managed by changing the likelihood of the risk
ML/TF risks may be managed by changing the consequences of the risk
ML/TF risks may be managed by sharing the risk with another party or parties
ML/TF risks may be managed by retaining the risk through informed decisions

sections.source_quote: According to ISO 31000: 2009 (International Organization for Standardization), risk may be managed or dealt with, as follows: avoiding the risk by deciding not to start or continue with the activity that gives rise to the risk; accepting or increasing the risk in order to pursue an opportunity; removing the risk source; changing the likelihood; changing the consequences; sharing the risk with another party or parties (including contracts and risk financing); retaining the risk by informed decision.

sections.heading: These actions apply to ML/TF risk, as with other risks.  An accountable institution's
sections.title: Risk appetite in ML/TF risk management
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must define their risk appetite for ML/TF risks, which determines how they allocate resources to mitigate risks, tolerate certain risks, and decide when to avoid or terminate risks. Risk appetite can vary across different types of risks and business contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes that institutions must define their risk appetite for ML/TF risks, which is critical for data transfers as it influences how institutions manage and mitigate risks associated with cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions' risk appetite determines the extent to which they will apply resources to treat (mitigate) risks
Accountable institutions' risk appetite determines the extent to which they may tolerate certain risks
Accountable institutions' risk appetite determines instances when they will avoid or terminate risks
Accountable institutions' risk appetite can differ in relation to different types of risk (e.g. money laundering risk as opposed to terrorist financing risk)
Accountable institutions' risk appetite can differ in relation to risks arising in different contexts

sections.source_quote: These actions apply to ML/TF risk, as with other risks. An accountable institution's risk appetite is a key determining factor in relation to its risk management decisions, in particular the extent to which it will apply resources to treat (mitigate) risks, the extent to which it may tolerate certain risks and the instances when it will avoid or terminate risks. An accountable institution's risk appetite can also differ in relation to different types of risk (e.g. money laundering risk as opposed to terrorist financing risk) or risks arising in different contexts.

sections.heading: Management and mitigation of ML/TF risks most probably will entail the treatment

sections.summary: Accountable institutions must develop systems and controls to manage identified ML/TF risks. These systems should include relevant risk mitigation measures tailored to the specific nature of each risk, such as customer due diligence, monitoring business relationships, and managing delivery channels. The controls must be comprehensive and applicable to the institution's operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to implement risk-specific controls for ML/TF risks, which impacts how data transfer and processing activities are structured to mitigate cross-border financial crime risks.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must develop systems and controls to manage identified ML/TF risks

Mechanisms include monitoring business relationships with clients
Mechanisms include managing delivery channels for products and services

sections.source_quote: 25. Management and mitigation of ML/TF risks most probably will entail the treatment of identified risks within an accountable institution. sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies that ML/TF risk management must be integrated with institutional operations, affecting how data transfers and processing activities are evaluated for compliance with financial crime prevention.

sections.source_quote: 26. The concepts of "ML/TF risk" and "ML/TF risk management" must always be contextualised within the particular business of an accountable institution, and as having an impact on the operational, line management and strategic objectives of that accountable institution.

sections.heading: The application of risk management systems and controls must be commensurate
sections.title: Risk management commensurate with assessed risk levels
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must apply risk management systems and controls proportional to the assessed risk levels. The extent of risk mitigation mechanisms must be relevant to the consequences of the risk in specific scenarios, considering the likelihood and impact of the risk.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires risk-based proportionality in data transfer and processing activities to ensure that financial crime risks are appropriately mitigated without excessive resource allocation.

requirements.text: The application of risk management systems and controls must be commensurate with the extent of assessed risks

sections.heading: The process to manage ML/TF risk is a continuous cycle.  Accountable institutions
sections.title: Continuous cycle of ML/TF risk management
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must maintain a continuous cycle of ML/TF risk management that adapts to changing circumstances such as emerging threats, product innovations, new markets, and client changes. The systems and controls must remain adequate and consistently adhered to within the institution.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates ongoing review of ML/TF risk management systems to ensure they remain effective as data transfer and processing activities evolve, critical for cross-border financial compliance.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions should ensure their ML/TF risk management systems and controls remain adequate in view of changing circumstances
Accountable institutions should ensure their ML/TF risk management systems and controls are adhered to within the institution

sections.source_quote: 28. The process to manage ML/TF risk is a continuous cycle. Accountable institutions should satisfy themselves that their ML/FT risk management systems and controls remain adequate in view of changing circumstances relating to emerging threats and vulnerabilities, product innovations, new target markets, changes in circumstances of individual clients or classes of clients, etc. Accountable institutions should also ensure that their ML/FT risk management systems and controls are adhered to within that institution.

sections.heading: This means that accountable institutions should reassess ML/TF risks, in particular
sections.title: Regular reassessment of ML/TF risks
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must regularly reassess ML/TF risks, particularly residual risks after applying risk management systems and controls. They should then review the continued adequacy of their risk management systems and controls based on these reassessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires periodic reassessment of ML/TF risks to ensure data transfer and processing activities remain compliant with evolving financial crime threats.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions should reassess ML/TF risks, in particular residual risks after the application of ML/FT risk management systems and controls
Institutions should review the continued adequacy of ML/FT risk management systems and controls based on regular assessments

sections.source_quote: 29. This means that accountable institutions should reassess ML/TF risks, in particular residual risks after the application of ML/FT risk management systems and controls, at regular intervals. The institutions should then review the continued adequacy of its ML/FT risk management systems and controls on the basis of these regular assessments.

sections.heading: By applying a risk-based approach accountable institutions are able to ensure that
sections.title: Risk-based approach for effective ML/TF risk management
sections.summary: By applying a risk-based approach, accountable institutions ensure that ML/TF risk mitigation measures are commensurate with identified risks, directing resources to the highest priority risks. This approach allows flexibility in client verification and promotes financial inclusion while maintaining AML/CFT objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to use risk-based approaches for data transfers and processing to balance financial crime prevention with client inclusion, critical for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must apply a risk-based approach to ensure measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are commensurate with identified risks
Measures must be directed in accordance with priorities to ensure greatest risks receive highest attention
Accountable institutions should ensure alignment between Treating Customer Fairly principles and ML/TF risk guidance

By applying a risk-based approach accountable institutions are able to ensure that measures to prevent or mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing are commensurate with the risks identified. This will ensure that resources are directed in accordance with priorities, so that the greatest risks receive the highest attention.

sections.heading: The risk-based approach further allows accountable institutions to simplify the due
sections.title: Simplified due diligence for low-risk ML/TF exposures
sections.summary: Accountable institutions can simplify their due diligence measures when assessing money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks are deemed low. This allows them to avoid rigid regulatory requirements and exemptions, instead using their internal AML and CFT compliance programs to determine appropriate compliance steps. The approach provides flexibility based on the institution's risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows institutions to reduce compliance burden for low-risk transfers by avoiding standardized checks, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border financial data flows with minimal ML/TF risk.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions may simplify due diligence measures for low ML/TF risk exposures
Accountable institutions must determine appropriate compliance steps based on internal AML and CFT compliance programs

sections.source_quote: The risk-based approach further allows accountable institutions to simplify the due diligence measures applied where they assess ML/TF risks to be lower. Instead of relying on rigid requirements in regulations and exemptions granted at the executive level, accountable institutions will have greater discretion to determine the appropriate compliance steps to be taken in given instances, in accordance with their internal AML and CFT compliance and risk management programmes.

sections.heading: Different industries or sectors have different exposures to ML/TF risk.  The Centre

sections.summary: Different industries and sectors face varying levels of money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risk. sections.dtia_summary: Indicates potential for tailored risk assessments in cross-border financial data transfers, but current DTIAs must follow general risk-based approach without sector-specific rules.

requirements.text: FIC will consider specific guidance for industry or sector-specific ML/TF risk challenges

sections.heading: Assessing ML/TF risk requires accountable institutions to identify the ML/TF risks
sections.title: ML/TF risk identification and analysis requirements
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must identify and analyze money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks within their businesses, including assessing the likelihood and impact of these risks. They must establish adequate processes proportional to their size and complexity to effectively identify and assess these risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to conduct thorough risk assessments for cross-border data transfers, ensuring ML/TF risks are properly evaluated before data flows across borders.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must identify ML/TF risks in their businesses
Accountable institutions must analyze ML/TF risks to understand their impact
Accountable institutions must assess likelihood and impact of ML/TF risks
Accountable institutions must have adequate processes proportional to size and complexity for risk identification and assessment

sections.source_quote: Assessing ML/TF risk requires accountable institutions to identify the ML/TF risks they may face in the context of their businesses and to analyse these with a view to understand how the identified ML/TF risks affects them. The likelihood of a risk transpiring as well as the impact of such a risk should also be considered by the accountable institution. This implies that accountable institutions must have adequate processes, proportionate to their size and complexity, to identify and assess ML/TF risks.

sections.heading: The mechanisms used in a particular accountable institution to assess ML/TF risk
sections.title: Proportionate risk assessment mechanisms
sections.summary: The mechanisms used by accountable institutions to assess money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks must be proportionate to the institution's size and complexity. This means the risk assessment process can range from simple to highly sophisticated depending on the institution's scale and the nature of its products and services.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures that cross-border data transfers are assessed with risk-appropriate measures, preventing overly complex or simplistic approaches that could compromise data security.

requirements.text: Risk assessment mechanisms must be proportionate to the size and complexity of the institution

sections.source_quote: The mechanisms used in a particular accountable institution to assess ML/TF risk must be proportionate to the size and complexity of the institution. The risk assessment process therefore might be quite simple or very sophisticated depending on the size and structure of the accountable institution and the nature and range of products and services it offers.
sections.relevance_reason: proportionate risk assessment for financial data

sections.heading: The processes within accountable institutions to identify and assess ML/TF risks
sections.title: Factors for ML/TF risk assessment
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must consider various factors that indicate increased threats or vulnerabilities to money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) in their risk assessment processes. These factors, which have been shown to be more easily abused by criminals, must be integrated into the institution's risk evaluation framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to identify and evaluate specific risk indicators for cross-border data transfers, ensuring that high-risk scenarios are properly addressed.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must consider factors indicative of increased ML/TF threats or vulnerabilities

sections.source_quote: The processes within accountable institutions to identify and assess ML/TF risks must take account of a range of factors which may be indicative of greater or lesser threats and vulnerabilities to money laundering and terrorist financing in a given scenario. Factors that are indicative of increased threats or vulnerabilities have been shown, based on previous experience, to lend themselves more readily to abuse by criminals.
sections.relevance_reason: AML risk assessment factors

sections.heading: Accountable institutions may have access to and may use various databases to
sections.title: Use of external databases for ML/TF risk assessment
sections.summary: Accountable institutions may access and use various databases to assess money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks related to their clients. They must determine the weight given to national-level ML/TF risks in jurisdictions where they operate and describe these processes in their Risk Management and Compliance Programme (RMCP).
sections.dtia_summary: Involves using external data sources for cross-border risk assessments, requiring institutions to document how they weigh national risks when transferring financial data internationally.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions may access and use databases to assess ML/TF risks

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions may have access to and may use various databases to assess the ML/TF risks relating to their clients. The context within which accountable institutions assess ML/TF risks is also influenced by ML/TF risks that are identified at a national level in all the jurisdictions where they operate. Accountable institutions should therefore determine in their risk assessment processes what weight they give to risks identified at national level in the relevant jurisdictions where they operate. The sources of information on risk indicators and the processes for their consideration should be described in an accountable institution's Risk Management and Compliance Programme (RMCP).

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must maintain knowledge of their clients

sections.source_quote: Customer due diligence (CDD) refers to the knowledge that an accountable institution has about its client and the institution's understanding of the business that the client is conducting with it.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data CDD obligations for institutions

sections.heading: CDD measures, if properly implemented, enables an accountable institution to
sections.title: Benefits of customer due diligence for financial institutions
sections.summary: Properly implemented customer due diligence measures help financial institutions better manage client relationships and identify potential illicit activities. This is a key component of effective money laundering and terrorism financing prevention frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section highlights the importance of customer due diligence in detecting cross-border financial crimes, which affects data transfer assessments for high-risk client identification.
sections.source_quote: CDD measures, if properly implemented, enables an accountable institution to better manage its relationships with clients and to better identify possible attempts by clients to exploit the institution's products and services for illicit purposes. Requiring accountable institutions to apply CDD is a key component of a framework to combat money laundering and terrorism financing effectively.
sections.relevance_reason: customer due diligence for financial institutions

sections.heading: Previously accountable institutions were required to establish and verify the
sections.title: Transition from client identification to customer due diligence
sections.summary: Previously, accountable institutions were required to establish and verify client identities under the MLTFC Regulations. The FIC Act now expands this to include comprehensive customer due diligence, which has led to significant amendments in regulations and exemptions related to client identification and verification.
sections.dtia_summary: This section describes the shift from basic client identification to comprehensive customer due diligence, impacting how financial institutions handle client data across borders.
sections.source_quote: Previously accountable institutions were required to establish and verify the identity of a client in accordance with the MLTFC Regulations. The principle of client identification and verification is now expanded significantly with the introduction of the obligation to conduct CDD. As a result, the regulations and exemptions relating to client identification and verification have been amended significantly to align with the amendments to the FIC Act, with most of the regulations having been repealed and exemptions having been withdrawn.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data compliance with customer due diligence

sections.heading: This, combined with the obligation to apply a risk-based approach (as discussed
sections.title: Flexibility in customer due diligence implementation
sections.summary: Accountable institutions have greater flexibility to determine the appropriate client identification and verification methods under a risk-based approach. This includes choosing the type of information and verification methods, rather than following rigid steps from previous regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows financial institutions to adapt client identification and verification methods for cross-border data transfers based on risk assessments.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions have greater discretion to determine appropriate compliance steps
Accountable institutions may choose the type of information for client identity verification
Accountable institutions may choose the means of verifying client identities

sections.source_quote: This, combined with the obligation to apply a risk-based approach (as discussed in Chapter 1 above), gives accountable institutions greater discretion to determine the appropriate compliance steps to be taken in given instances. This means that accountable institutions now have the flexibility to choose the type of information by means of which it will establish clients' identities and also the means of verification of clients' identities, instead of following the rigid steps provided for in the MLTFC Regulations.
sections.relevance_reason: risk-based approach for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Accountable institutions should use the findings from their risk assessment to
sections.title: CDD measures based on risk assessment for client relationships
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must use their risk assessment findings to determine the appropriate level and type of customer due diligence (CDD) measures for clients, business relationships, and single transactions. They must also define when persons are considered prospective clients for CDD obligations. The Risk Management and Compliance Plan (RMCP) must specify CDD measures and how they are adjusted based on money laundering and terrorist financing risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to establish CDD measures tied to risk levels, which impacts how data is collected and processed for client relationships across borders.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions should use risk assessment findings to decide CDD level and type for clients, business relationships, and single transactions
Accountable institutions should determine when persons are considered prospective clients for CDD obligations
RMCP must describe CDD measures applied and how they are attenuated or intensified based on ML/TF risks

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions should use the findings from their risk assessment to decide on the appropriate level and type of CDD that they will apply to a client (or business relationship and single transactions). Accountable institutions should also determine when they consider persons to be prospective clients to whom their CDD measures apply. An accountable institution's RMCP must describe the CDD measures which the institution applies and how these measures are attenuated or intensified on the basis of ML/TF risks.
sections.relevance_reason: CDD measures for financial data compliance

sections.heading: A business relationship is defined in the FIC Act as an arrangement between a
sections.title: Definition of business relationship in financial context
sections.summary: A business relationship is defined as a recurring arrangement between a client and an accountable institution for transaction purposes, with a time duration element. This definition establishes the scope of obligations under the Financial Intelligence Centre Act for ongoing client engagements.
sections.dtia_summary: This definition clarifies what constitutes a business relationship, which is critical for determining when data transfer obligations apply across borders.

requirements.text: A business relationship is an arrangement between a client and an accountable institution for the purpose of concluding transactions on a regular basis
A business relationship entails an element of time duration to the engagement with the client

sections.source_quote: A business relationship is defined in the FIC Act as an arrangement between a client and an accountable institution for the purpose of concluding transactions on a regular basis. A business relationship therefore entails an element of a time duration to the engagement with the client.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines business relationship scope for financial data

sections.heading: Accountable institutions need to determine what constitutes a business
sections.title: Determination of business relationship scope for compliance
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must define what constitutes a business relationship and transaction within their specific business context to comply with the Financial Intelligence Centre Act's obligations. This ensures alignment with the Act's requirements for ongoing client engagements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to clarify business relationship definitions for their specific operations, which affects how data is processed across borders.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions need to determine what constitutes a business relationship
Accountable institutions need to determine what constitutes a transaction

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions need to determine what constitutes a business relationship as well as a transaction in the context of their particular business for purposes of complying with the obligations of the FIC Act in as far as it applies to a business relationship.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines business relationship scope for compliance

sections.heading: The manner and the point in time at which an accountable institution determines
sections.title: Timing of client determination for FIC obligations
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must specify in their Risk Management and Compliance Plan (RMCP) when they determine a person is a prospective or client, for both business relationships and single transactions. This timing affects when CDD and other FIC Act obligations commence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies when client determination occurs, which is critical for timing data transfer obligations across borders.

requirements.text: The manner and point in time at which an accountable institution determines that a person is a prospective client or client should be spelled out in the RMCP
Accountable institutions should determine who is to be regarded as a prospective client and client in their particular business

sections.source_quote: The manner and the point in time at which an accountable institution determines that a person is a prospective client or a client for the purposes of determining when the obligations of the FIC Act commences should be spelled out in an accountable institution's RMCP, both in respect of a business relationship and a single transaction. The accountable institution should therefore determine, taking into account its particular business, who is to be regarded as a prospective client and client in order to apply the CDD and other requirements in terms of the FIC Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Timing of client determination affects CDD obligations

sections.heading: The FIC Act defines a single transaction as a transaction other than a transaction
sections.title: Definition of single transaction threshold
sections.summary: A single transaction is defined as any transaction not part of a business relationship where the value exceeds R5,000.00 (subject to regulatory adjustments). This definition applies to occasional or one-off transactions with no expectation of recurrence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the single transaction threshold, which impacts how data is processed for one-off transactions across borders.

requirements.text: A single transaction is a transaction other than one concluded in the course of a business relationship and where the value is not less than R5 000.00
The transaction value threshold is determined by the Minister of Finance in Regulations
Single transactions are occasional or once-off business with no expectation of recurrence

sections.source_quote: The FIC Act defines a single transaction as a transaction other than a transaction concluded in the course of a business relationship and where the value of the transaction is not less than R5 000.00 (the amount is determined by the Minister of Finance in the Regulations). This can be described as occasional or once-off business where there is no expectation on the part of the accountable institution or the client that the engagements would recur over a period of time.
sections.relevance_reason: Definition of transaction threshold for financial transactions

sections.heading: Institutions need to determine what constitutes a single transaction in the context
sections.title: Determination of single transaction scope for compliance
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must define what constitutes a single transaction within their specific business context to comply with the Financial Intelligence Centre Act's obligations. This ensures alignment with the Act's requirements for one-off transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to clarify single transaction definitions for their operations, which affects how data is processed across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions need to determine what constitutes a single transaction in the context of their particular business

sections.source_quote: Institutions need to determine what constitutes a single transaction in the context of their particular business for purposes of complying with the obligations of the FIC Act in as far as it applies to single transactions.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines single transaction scope for compliance

sections.heading: Accountable institutions are not required to carry out the full scope of CDD
sections.title: Single transactions below threshold exempt from full CDD but require basic identity info
sections.summary: Accountable institutions do not need to fully implement customer due diligence (CDD) measures for clients making single transactions below the Minister of Finance's set value threshold. However, they must still obtain at least basic client identity information such as full name and identity number, even if verification is not required. The institution must record this information in their Risk Management and Compliance Plan (RMCP) to comply with section 20A of the FIC Act, which prohibits dealing with anonymous or falsely named clients regardless of transaction value.
sections.dtia_summary: Single transactions below threshold exempt from full CDD but require basic identity information to avoid prohibited transactions with anonymous or fictitious clients.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must obtain at least some client identity information such as full name and identity number
Accountable institutions must record basic client identity information in their RMCP
Accountable institutions must not deal with anonymous clients or clients with apparent false/fictitious names

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions are not required to carry out the full scope of CDD measures in respect of clients conducting single transactions below the value to be set by the Minister of Finance in the MLTFC Regulations. However, the threshold for single transactions does not apply to the obligations set out in section 20A of the FIC Act.
sections.relevance_reason: CDD requirements for transactions

sections.heading: CDD starts with an accountable institution knowing the identity of its client.  In
sections.title: Accountable institutions must verify client identity during business relationships
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must establish and verify the identity of clients during business relationship onboarding or single transactions. This includes verifying the client and any representatives acting on their behalf. The verification process must ensure the institution has sufficient confidence in the client's identity based on their risk assessment for that specific engagement.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandatory client identity verification required for all transactions to prevent anonymous or fictitious client dealings.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must establish and verify client identity during business relationships or single transactions
Accountable institutions must verify the person representing the client if applicable
Accountable institutions must have confidence in client identity based on risk assessment

sections.source_quote: CDD starts with an accountable institution knowing the identity of its client. In terms of section 21 of the FIC Act an accountable institution must, in the course of establishing a business relationship or entering into a single transaction, establish and verify the identity of the client and, if applicable, the person representing the client as well as any other person on whose behalf the client is acting.
sections.relevance_reason: Client identity verification for data compliance

sections.heading: Establishing a client's identity entails that an accountable institution obtains a
sections.title: Client identity verification requires corroborating information with reliable sources
sections.summary: Establishing client identity requires obtaining a range of information from the client, typically during onboarding or engagement. Verification involves corroborating this information with reliable third-party sources to ensure accuracy and authenticity. This process is critical for confirming the client's identity beyond self-declared information.
sections.dtia_summary: Client identity verification must be corroborated with reliable third-party sources to prevent identity fraud.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must obtain a range of information about the client
Accountable institutions must corroborate client identity information with reliable third-party sources

sections.source_quote: Establishing a client's identity entails that an accountable institution obtains a range of information about the client. Verification of the client's identity entails that the accountable institution corroborates the person's identity information by comparing this information with information contained in documents or electronic data issued or created by reliable and independent third-party sources.
sections.relevance_reason: Client identity verification with third-party sources

sections.heading: As indicated in Chapter 1 above, large parts of the MLTFC Regulations, (in
sections.title: Accountable institutions must define client identity methods based on risk assessment
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must determine how they will establish and verify client identities, considering the assessed ML/TF risks associated with each business relationship or transaction. These decisions must be documented in the institution's Risk Management and Compliance Plan (RMCP) to ensure appropriate risk mitigation measures are applied based on the client's risk profile.
sections.dtia_summary: Client identity verification methods must be risk-based and documented in RMCP to align with ML/TF risk assessments.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must determine how they establish and verify client identities
Accountable institutions must consider assessed ML/TF risks when determining verification methods
Accountable institutions must document client identification and verification methods in RMCP

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions must now choose the type of information by means of which they will establish clients' identities and also the means of verification of clients' identities. The nature and extent of verification of clients' identities must be determined taking the assessed ML/TF risks associated with the relevant business relationship or single transaction into account.
sections.relevance_reason: Client identity verification for compliance

sections.heading: A natural person's identity can be determined by reference to a number of
sections.title: Basic identity attributes for natural persons include name, date of birth and unique identifier
sections.summary: Natural persons' identities can be determined using basic attributes such as full names, date of birth, and a unique government-issued identifier like an identity number for South African citizens or passport numbers for other nationalities. These basic attributes are expected to be used in all accountable institutions' identity verification processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Basic identity attributes (name, date of birth, unique identifier) are mandatory for natural person identification.

requirements.text: Basic identity attributes include full names, date of birth, and unique government-issued identifier

sections.source_quote: A natural person's identity can be determined by reference to a number of attributes. At the very basic level these attributes are the person's full names, date of birth and, in most cases, a unique identifying number issued by a government source (e.g. in the case of a South African citizen or resident, his/her identity number or, in the case of other natural persons, a passport number or numbers contained in asylum seeker or refugee permits, work permits, visitors' visas etc.).
sections.relevance_reason: Core identity data for compliance

sections.heading: Information about a natural person's identity may be supplemented by applying
sections.title: Additional identity attributes may be used based on risk mitigation needs
sections.summary: Accountable institutions may supplement basic identity information with additional attributes such as physical appearance, biometric data, place of birth, employment details, residential address, contact information, or tax numbers, depending on the level of risk mitigation required for the specific client engagement. The extent of additional information is determined by the institution's risk assessment.
sections.dtia_summary: Additional identity attributes can be used to enhance verification, but must align with the institution's risk mitigation needs.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions may supplement basic identity information with additional attributes
Additional attributes must be determined based on the institution's risk mitigation needs

sections.source_quote: Information about a natural person's identity may be supplemented by applying other attributes of a natural person including his/her physical appearance or other biometric information, place of birth, family circumstances, place of employment or business, residential address, contact particulars (e.g. telephone numbers, e-mail addresses, social media), contacts with the authorities (e.g. tax numbers) or with other accountable institutions.
sections.relevance_reason: Risk-based data collection for customer identity

sections.heading: Verification methods vary and are mostly dictated by the type of information used
sections.title: Verification methods depend on information type for identity confirmation
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must verify a person's identity using reliable, independent third-party sources, preferably the original information source. They must evaluate the adequacy of corroboration for identity attributes and determine the level of confidence in the verification method's accuracy. Institutions must ensure information sources have controls to maintain accuracy and avoid using client-generated information as reliable sources.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to validate identity through trusted sources, impacting cross-border data transfers by mandating reliable verification methods for international identity checks.

requirements.text: Verification must use information from reliable and independent third-party sources
Verification should use original source of information where possible
Institutions must evaluate adequacy of corroboration for identity attributes
Institutions must determine confidence level in verification methods
Information sources must have controls to ensure accuracy
Client-generated information is not considered reliable third-party sources

sections.source_quote: Verification methods vary and are mostly dictated by the type of information used to establish a person's identity in a given scenario. Regardless of the method applied, it is important that verification be done using information obtained from a reliable and independent third-party source and, as far as possible, the original source of the information.
sections.relevance_reason: Identity verification for personal data

sections.heading: Government issued or controlled sources of information such as various forms of
sections.title: Government-issued identity documents provide high confidence for verification
sections.summary: Accountable institutions should primarily use government-issued identity documents (e.g., passports, driver's licenses) as reliable sources for verifying basic identity attributes. They may only use alternative sources in exceptional cases where the original source is unavailable, but must be confident in managing money laundering and terrorist financing risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Prioritizes government-issued documents for cross-border identity verification, reducing reliance on potentially less reliable third-party data sources.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions should use government issued or controlled sources for basic identity verification
Accountable institutions may only use alternative sources in exceptional cases
Alternative sources must be used only when original source is unavailable
Institutions must be confident in managing ML/TF risks when using alternative sources

sections.source_quote: Government issued or controlled sources of information such as various forms of identity documents... are most often the original sources of information concerning the relevant attributes and therefore provide a high level of confidence as a means to corroborate a natural person's basic identity attributes.
sections.relevance_reason: ML/TF risk management for identity verification

sections.heading: Corroboration of a person's identity in relation to both basic and other identity
sections.title: Electronic footprint enhances identity verification across multiple sources
sections.summary: Accountable institutions can use electronic data sources to corroborate identity attributes, including information accumulated over time in an individual's electronic footprint. They should leverage multiple third-party data sources to verify identity, particularly when electronic information is available.
sections.dtia_summary: Encourages use of electronic footprints for cross-border identity verification, requiring institutions to validate identity through multiple data sources over time.

requirements.text: Corroboration of identity can be in documentary or electronic form
Institutions should use electronic information to corroborate against multiple third-party data sources

sections.source_quote: Corroboration of a person's identity in relation to both basic and other identity attributes can be in documentary or electronic form. Moreover, many of a person's identity attributes accumulate over time and can be found in the person's so-called 'electronic footprint'.
sections.relevance_reason: Identity verification using third-party data sources

sections.heading: Accountable institutions making use of electronic data sources to verify a
sections.title: Electronic data sources do not provide automatic regulatory immunity
sections.summary: Accountable institutions remain fully responsible for compliance with the FIC Act when using electronic data sources for identity verification. Electronic data sources do not provide automatic protection from regulatory action, and institutions must apply due diligence in selecting electronic verification solutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Emphasizes that electronic identity verification does not exempt institutions from regulatory compliance, critical for cross-border data transfers involving electronic identity checks.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions remain responsible for compliance with FIC Act when using electronic data sources
Electronic data sources do not provide automatic indemnity from regulatory action
Institutions must apply due diligence in choosing electronic verification solutions

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions making use of electronic data sources to verify a prospective client's identity remain responsible and accountable in their own capacity for compliance with the requirements of the FIC Act.
sections.relevance_reason: core data protection and cross-border compliance

sections.heading: If accountable institutions make use of electronic data sources they should apply
sections.title: Electronic sources must meet same reliability standards as documentary sources
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must treat electronic data sources with the same reliability standards as documentary sources. They must ensure electronic sources are reliable, independent third-party sources and the original information source where possible. Institutions must determine confidence levels in electronic sources and avoid solutions where clients can manipulate source information.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires electronic identity verification to meet documentary standards, ensuring consistent reliability for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Electronic sources must be reliable and independent third-party sources
Electronic sources should be original information source where possible
Institutions must determine confidence level in electronic sources
Solutions allowing client manipulation of source information are not credible

sections.source_quote: If accountable institutions make use of electronic data sources they should apply the same test in principle as in the case of documentary sources, i.e. that the sources should be reliable and independent third-party sources and as, far as possible, the original source of the relevant information.
sections.relevance_reason: Reliability standards for electronic data sources

sections.heading: A factor that may contribute to an accountable institution's confidence in
sections.title: Electronic footprint size affects confidence in identity verification
sections.summary: The size of a person's electronic footprint (depth, breadth, and quality of electronic information) influences an institution's confidence in electronic identity verification. Institutions should use information from multiple sources across time to increase confidence in verification results.
sections.dtia_summary: Highlights that electronic footprint diversity and temporal scope enhance cross-border identity verification confidence, critical for international data transfers.

requirements.text: Larger electronic footprint increases confidence in verification
Institutions should use information from multiple sources across time

sections.source_quote: A factor that may contribute to an accountable institution's confidence in corroboration based on electronic sources is the size of a person's 'electronic footprint' in relation to the depth, breadth and quality of electronic information.
sections.relevance_reason: tangential to data compliance

sections.heading: The means of verification and the sources of corroboration an accountable
sections.title: Verification methods depend on client identity reliance for ML/TF risk mitigation
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must use verification methods and corroboration sources that align with the extent of reliance on client identity verification to mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing risks. This applies to all cases where identity verification is part of the institution's risk assessment process. The verification approach must be proportionate to the specific risk profile of the transaction.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies that verification methods must be risk-based, requiring institutions to tailor their identity verification processes according to the level of reliance on client identity for ML/TF risk mitigation. This impacts cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions by necessitating proportionate verification measures.

requirements.text: Verification methods and corroboration sources depend on the extent of reliance on client identity verification for ML/TF risk mitigation

sections.source_quote: The means of verification and the sources of corroboration an accountable institution uses in a given case are dependent on the extent to which the institution relies on the verification of the client's identity as a means to mitigate ML/TF risk in that particular case (see the discussion on risk mitigation in Chapter 1 above).
sections.relevance_reason: AML verification methods affect data handling

sections.heading: Examples of sources of information that may corroborate a person's identity
sections.title: Examples of identity verification sources for individuals
sections.summary: Accountable institutions may use records from the Department of Home Affairs, Companies and Intellectual Property Commission, South African Revenue Service, eNaTIS, and Master of the High Court to corroborate an individual's identity. These sources provide verifiable evidence for identity verification purposes in financial transactions. The section also addresses how to establish the identity of legal entities, trusts, and partnerships.
sections.dtia_summary: This section lists specific government records that can be used for identity verification, which may impact cross-border data transfers by requiring institutions to validate identities using these sources. Institutions must ensure the sources are reliable and accessible for international transactions.

requirements.text: Records of the Department of Home Affairs may corroborate a person's identity
Records of the Companies and Intellectual Property Commission may corroborate a person's identity
Records of the South African Revenue Service may corroborate a person's identity
eNaTIS records may corroborate a person's identity
Records of the Master of the High Court may corroborate a person's identity

sections.source_quote: Examples of sources of information that may corroborate a person's identity attributes include records of the Department of Home Affairs, records of the Companies and Intellectual Property Commission, records of the South African Revenue Service, eNaTIS records and records of the Master of the High Court.
sections.relevance_reason: AML identity verification sources

sections.heading: Section 21 of the FIC Act (the requirement to establish and verify a client's identity)
sections.title: Identity verification for corporate clients and additional due diligence
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must verify the identity of corporate clients (legal persons, trusts, partnerships) and apply additional due diligence measures including business nature, ownership structure, and beneficial ownership verification. This extends the client identity verification requirements from natural persons to corporate entities and mandates specific verification steps for beneficial owners.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to verify corporate clients' identities and apply additional due diligence measures, which impacts cross-border data transfers by necessitating detailed verification of corporate entities' ownership structures and beneficial ownership.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must verify the identity of corporate clients including legal persons, trusts and partnerships
Accountable institutions must establish the nature of the client's business
Accountable institutions must establish the ownership and control structure of the client
Accountable institutions must establish the beneficial ownership of clients
Accountable institutions must take reasonable steps to verify the identity of beneficial owners

sections.source_quote: Section 21 of the FIC Act (the requirement to establish and verify a client's identity) also applies to clients who are not natural persons acting in their personal capacity. Clients of this nature are referred to as corporate vehicles and include legal persons, trusts and partnerships.
sections.relevance_reason: AML due diligence for corporate clients

sections.heading: The requirements set out in sections 21 and 21B of the FIC Act apply whether the
sections.title: Applicability of FIC Act identity verification requirements globally
sections.summary: The identity verification requirements under sections 21 and 21B of the FIC Act apply to both South African and non-South African legal persons, partnerships, and trusts. This ensures consistent identity verification practices regardless of the jurisdiction where the client entity is incorporated or formed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section confirms that identity verification requirements apply globally, meaning institutions must comply with these rules for clients from any country, which affects cross-border data transfers by requiring consistent verification standards internationally.

requirements.text: The requirements set out in sections 21 and 21B of the FIC Act apply whether the legal person, partnership or trust is incorporated or originated in South Africa or elsewhere

sections.source_quote: The requirements set out in sections 21 and 21B of the FIC Act apply whether the legal person, partnership or trust or similar arrangement between natural persons is incorporated or originated in South Africa or elsewhere.
sections.relevance_reason: AML identity verification globally

sections.heading: A legal person is defined in the FIC Act as any person, other than a natural person,
sections.title: Definition of legal persons in FIC Act
sections.summary: A legal person in the FIC Act refers to any entity other than a natural person that establishes a business relationship or enters into a transaction with an accountable institution. This includes companies, close corporations, foreign companies, and other corporate arrangements but excludes trusts, partnerships, and sole proprietors.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines legal persons, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it clarifies which entities require identity verification and additional due diligence measures under the FIC Act.

requirements.text: A legal person is defined as any person other than a natural person that establishes a business relationship or enters into a single transaction with an accountable institution

sections.source_quote: A legal person is defined in the FIC Act as any person, other than a natural person, that establishes a business relationship or enters into a single transaction with an accountable institution and includes a person incorporated as a company, close corporation, foreign company or any other form of corporate arrangement or association but excludes a trust, partnership or sole proprietor.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines legal persons relevant to data transfers

sections.heading: Examples of  attributes which describe a legal person's identity include:
sections.title: Identity attributes for legal persons
sections.summary: Legal persons must provide specific identity attributes including incorporation name, form, registration number, registered office address, governing powers, directors/members, senior management, trading name, business address, and tax numbers. These attributes are essential for verifying legal persons' identities and ensuring compliance with identity verification requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section lists specific identity attributes required for legal persons, which impacts cross-border data transfers by mandating detailed information about legal entities to ensure accurate identity verification across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Legal persons must provide the name under which they have been incorporated
Legal persons must provide their form (e.g., company or close corporation)
Legal persons must provide their registration number if applicable
Legal persons must provide their registered office address
Legal persons must provide the powers that regulate and bind them
Legal persons must provide their directors or members
Legal persons must provide their senior management (e.g., chief executive officer)
Legal persons must provide their trading name if different from incorporation name
Legal persons must provide their business address if different from registered office address
Legal persons must provide their income tax or value added tax numbers

sections.source_quote: Examples of attributes which describe a legal person's identity include: The name under which the legal person has been incorporated; Its form, e.g. whether it is a company or a close corporation; The registration number under which it is incorporated, if applicable; The address of its registered office; The powers that regulate and bind the legal person; Its directors or members, as may be applicable; Its senior management e.g. its chief executive officer; Its trading name if it is different from the name under which it has been incorporated; Its business address if it is different from the address of its registered office; and Its income tax or value added tax numbers.
sections.relevance_reason: Identity verification for legal persons

sections.heading: The attributes used to establish a legal person's identity must be sufficient to prove
sections.title: Legal person identity verification sufficiency for ML/TF risk mitigation
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must use identity attributes sufficient to prove a legal person's existence and identity. The extent of verification depends on the institution's reliance on client identity verification to mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing risk. This must be documented in the institution's Risk Management and Control Plan (RMCP).
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to verify legal person identity attributes with risk-appropriate measures for cross-border data transfers involving legal entities.

requirements.text: Identity attributes must be sufficient to prove legal person existence and identity
Verification extent depends on ML/TF risk mitigation reliance
Verification measures must be documented in RMCP

sections.source_quote: The attributes used to establish a legal person's identity must be sufficient to prove the existence of the legal person and describe the legal person's identity. The nature and amount of the attributes which an accountable institution applies in a given case are dependent on the extent to which the institution relies on the verification of the client's identity as a means to mitigate ML/TF risk in that particular case (see the discussion on risk mitigation in Chapter 1 above). This must be described in the institution's RMCP.
sections.relevance_reason: AML/KYC verification requirements for legal persons

sections.heading: As in the case of natural persons, it is important that verification be done using
sections.title: Legal person identity verification using reliable third-party sources
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must verify legal person identity using information from reliable and independent third-party sources, preferably original sources. Public sector institutions' information, such as the Companies and Intellectual Property Commission, should be prioritized for identity verification. This applies to legal persons as it does to natural persons.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to use verifiable third-party sources for legal person identity verification, impacting cross-border data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Verification must use information from reliable and independent third-party sources
Prefer original source information to corroborate legal person identity

sections.heading: Accountable institutions must keep transaction records of single transactions and

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must keep transaction records of single transactions

sections.heading: This means that the accountable institution must keep records of every transaction

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must keep records of every transaction with a client

sections.heading: The FIC Act is not prescriptive as to the manner in which records must be kept.
sections.title: Record-keeping flexibility for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section states that the Financial Intelligence Centre Act does not specify how records must be stored. sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that financial institutions have flexibility in record storage methods, but must ensure records remain accessible for regulatory purposes. It does not impose specific technical requirements for data transfers.

sections.heading: There are many examples of mechanisms which may be used for the storage of
