sections.heading: Regardless of the manner in which records are kept, accountable institutions must
sections.title: Record accessibility and availability requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial institutions ensure records are accessible without obstruction, readily available to the Financial Intelligence Centre and supervisory bodies when required, capable of being reproduced in legible format, and if stored off-site, details of the third party must be provided to the Centre and supervisory bodies. These requirements apply regardless of storage method.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes critical requirements for record accessibility and availability, particularly relevant for data transfers involving off-site storage. It mandates that records must be accessible to South African authorities even if stored internationally.

requirements.text: The accountable institution must have free and easy (unencumbered) access to the relevant records
The records must be readily available to the Centre and the relevant supervisory body when required
The records must be capable of being reproduced in a legible format
If the records are stored off-site, the Centre and the relevant supervisory body must be provided with the details of the third party storing the records

sections.source_quote: 170. Regardless of the manner in which records are kept, accountable institutions must ensure that the following principles are met: The accountable institution must have free and easy (in other words unencumbered) access to the relevant records; The records must be readily available to the Centre and the relevant supervisory body when required; The records must be capable of being reproduced in a legible format and If the records are stored off-site the Centre and the relevant supervisory body must be provided with the details of the third party storing the records.

sections.heading: It is advisable that records include details that will assist in the identification of the

requirements.text: Records should include reference numbers on documents or letters
Records should include relevant dates such as issue or expiry
Records should include details of the issuer or writer

sections.heading: Accountable institutions must ensure that records are tamper proof and that there
sections.title: Record tamper-proofing and security for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section requires financial institutions to ensure records are tamper-proof and that safeguards are in place to prevent unauthorized electronic access to information. This applies to electronic records and focuses on data security and integrity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates tamper-proof records and security measures for electronic data, which is critical for data transfers involving sensitive financial information.

requirements.text: Records must be tamper-proof

sections.source_quote: 172. Accountable institutions must ensure that records are tamper proof and that there are safeguards in place to prevent the unauthorised access to information stored electronically.

sections.heading: Accountable institutions which operate in groups of companies may implement
sections.title: Group-wide record-keeping for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section allows financial institutions operating in groups to implement group-wide record-keeping policies, including centralized storage. It mandates that centralized storage outside South Africa must not impede access to records by South African authorities, and records must be kept in South Africa if restrictions in another jurisdiction would prevent such access.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses cross-border record storage for group entities, requiring that records must be accessible to South African authorities even when stored internationally, with specific provisions for data transfers.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions operating in groups may implement group-wide policies on record-keeping which may include centralised storage of records

sections.source_quote: 173. Accountable institutions which operate in groups of companies may implement group-wide policies on record-keeping which may include centralised storage of records. Accountable institutions should bear in mind that the principles mentioned in paragraph 170 above also apply where record keeping is centralised within a group. This is particularly important if the location of the centralised storage of records is outside of South Africa. No secrecy provisions or data protection legislation should restrict the South African based accountable institution(s)' free and easy access to the relevant records, or result in the records not being readily available to the Centre or relevant South African supervisory bodies or law enforcement authorities. If restrictions exist in another jurisdiction, where records are kept which would impede such access to the records, the relevant records or copies thereof must be kept in South Africa.

sections.summary: Accountable institutions using commercial third-party services or intra-group centralised data storage must conduct regular assessments of service providers and test controls to ensure they can access and retrieve data as required by the FIC Act. These assessments provide assurance to supervisory bodies about data access capabilities. The requirement applies to institutions handling financial data under the FIC Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to validate third-party service providers' data access capabilities through regular assessments and testing, critical for ensuring data transfer integrity in cross-border contexts.

Assessments must provide assurance to supervisory bodies about data access capabilities

sections.heading: Records of a transaction or activity which gave rise to a report contemplated in

sections.heading: Section 42 of the FIC Act places an obligation on accountable institutions to
sections.title: Risk-based compliance programme requirements

The RMCP must include policies, procedures, systems and controls to ensure effective risk-based compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to implement a risk-based compliance programme for money laundering and terrorist financing, critical for data processing and transfer safeguards.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must develop a Risk Management and Compliance Programme

The RMCP must be sufficient to counter money laundering and terrorist financing risks

sections.heading: The board of directors or senior management must create a culture of compliance

This culture must ensure staff adhere to legal obligations and regulatory requirements. The board or senior management is responsible for this implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes the need for a strong compliance culture in financial institutions, which affects data transfer practices by requiring staff awareness and adherence to AML/CFT regulations across borders.

RMCP must include escalation procedures for decision-making to higher seniority levels
RMCP must ensure money laundering risks are integrated into daily operations

sections.source_quote: 185. An accountable institution's RMCP must always be commensurate with the size and complexity of the institution and the nature of its business. This implies that a RMCP for an accountable institution which does not provide a wide range of products or services or deal with a diverse range of clients could be relatively simple while that of a complex financial institution would be expected to be much more complex. An accountable institution is required to indicate in its RMCP if any of the elements described in section 42 of the FIC Act do not apply to that particular institution. sections.heading: It is important that the content of an accountable institution's RMCP is

sections.heading: The FIC Act places the responsibility to administer the targeted financial sanctions

sections.dtia_summary: This section prohibits cross-border data transfers involving sanctioned entities, requiring institutions to screen clients against sanctions lists to avoid transactions with restricted parties.

Accountable institutions must not provide financial services to sanctioned persons or entities
The status quo of assets must be maintained for sanctioned persons or entities
The Minister of Finance may permit specific financial services for sanctioned persons or entities

sections.source_quote: The acquisition, collection or use of the property of persons or an entity whose names appear in the sanctions is prohibited. This includes the provision of financial services and products to those persons or entities.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial sanctions impact cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Accountable institutions must report to the Centre, the property in the accountable
sections.title: Accountable institutions must report sanctioned property
sections.summary: This section requires accountable institutions to report to the Centre any property in their possession or control that is owned or controlled by sanctioned persons or entities. This reporting is critical for monitoring and enforcing sanctions compliance across financial transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates reporting of sanctioned property, which impacts DTIAs by requiring institutions to identify and report data transfers involving sanctioned entities.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must report property in their possession or control owned by sanctioned persons or entities

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions must report to the Centre, the property in the accountable institution's possession or under its control which is owned or controlled by or on behalf of a person or an entity identified in the sanctions list.
sections.relevance_reason: Sanctions reporting for financial data

sections.heading: Accountable institutions must be able to determine whether they have a sanctioned
sections.title: Accountable institutions must screen clients against sanctions lists
sections.summary: This section mandates that accountable institutions must determine whether they have sanctioned persons or entities as clients or prospective clients. This requires screening clients and prospective clients against sanctions lists during client onboarding and periodically as sanctions lists are updated.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires screening of clients against sanctions lists, directly impacting DTIAs by ensuring data transfers do not involve sanctioned entities.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must determine whether they have sanctioned persons or entities as clients
Accountable institutions must screen prospective clients against sanctions lists

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions must be able to determine whether they have a sanctioned person or entity as a client or whether a prospective client is a sanctioned person or entity in order to determine their exposure to TFS-related obligations.
sections.relevance_reason: Sanctions screening for financial clients

sections.heading: Accountable institutions must therefore determine the likelihood that their client
sections.title: Determine likelihood of sanctioned persons in client base
sections.summary: Accountable institutions must assess the probability that their clients or prospective clients include sanctioned persons or entities. This assessment helps institutions allocate resources for screening purposes. Institutions with foreign clients face higher vulnerability to sanctions-related risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to evaluate cross-border client risks before transferring data involving international clients. This affects data transfer assessments for financial institutions operating globally.

requirements.text: Accountable institutions must determine the likelihood that their client base may include sanctioned persons or entities
Accountable institutions must determine the likelihood that prospective clients may include sanctioned persons or entities
Accountable institutions must allocate resources to screen for sanctioned persons or entities

sections.source_quote: Accountable institutions must therefore determine the likelihood that their client base and intended target market may include sanctioned persons or entities.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border sanctions risk assessment for financial data

sections.heading: Accountable institutions should be mindful of the fact that failure to comply with
sections.title: Criminal liability for non-compliance with sanctions screening
sections.summary: Failure to comply with financial sanctions screening obligations constitutes a criminal offence under section 49A of the FIC Act. Reliance on commercial screening tools or low risk assessments does not provide legal defences against criminal charges. Accountable institutions must ensure robust compliance mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly impacts DTIA by establishing criminal consequences for inadequate sanctions screening, requiring institutions to implement rigorous due diligence processes for cross-border data transfers involving sanctioned entities.

requirements.text: Failure to comply with TFS obligations is a criminal offence under section 49A of the FIC Act
Reliance on commercially available screening capabilities does not constitute a defence against criminal charges
Perceived low risk of sanctions exposure does not constitute a defence against criminal charges

sections.source_quote: failure to comply with TFS obligations is a criminal offence under section 49A of the FIC Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal liability for financial sanctions screening

sections.heading: The Centre will maintain an updated sanctions list which will be available on its
sections.title: Publicly available sanctions list with identity details
sections.summary: The Financial Intelligence Centre maintains an updated sanctions list accessible online, which includes identity details of persons and entities listed in official notices. This list reflects current sanctions status and is updated to reflect changes in the sanctions regime.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a public sanctions list that institutions must use for cross-border data screening, directly affecting DTIA requirements for sanctions risk assessment in international transactions.

requirements.text: The Centre maintains an updated sanctions list
The sanctions list is available on the Centre's website
The sanctions list reflects identity particulars of persons and entities in official notices

sections.source_quote: The Centre will maintain an updated sanctions list which will be available on its website and which will reflect available identity particulars of persons and entities contained in notices published by the Director.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial sanctions list impacts cross-border financial data transfers

sections.heading: The FIC Act allows the Minister of Finance to permit a sanctioned person or entity
sections.title: Basic living expenses exception for sanctioned persons
sections.summary: The Minister of Finance may permit sanctioned persons or entities to access financial services or deal with affected property for basic living expenses. This exception allows limited financial transactions to maintain essential needs, subject to specific conditions and detailed documentation requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Creates a specific exception for sanctioned persons to maintain basic financial services, requiring DTIA to assess whether data transfers support essential living expenses without triggering sanctions.

requirements.text: The Minister of Finance may permit sanctioned persons or entities to conduct financial services
The Minister of Finance may permit sanctioned persons or entities to deal with affected property
The permission may specify exact types of expenses covered
The permission may specify amounts of expenses
The permission may specify funds or property sources for expenses

sections.source_quote: The FIC Act allows the Minister of Finance to permit a sanctioned person or entity to conduct financial services or deal with property affected by a sanction in order to allow such a person or entity access to certain basic living expenses.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial exception for sanctioned persons

sections.heading: The Minister of Finance may also permit the provision of financial services or the
sections.title: Business necessity exceptions for sanctioned persons
sections.summary: The Minister of Finance may permit sanctioned persons or entities to maintain financial services or deal with affected property for business necessities unrelated to basic living expenses. This includes interest accruals and contractual obligations that would otherwise be prejudiced by sanctions.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows limited exceptions for sanctioned persons to maintain essential business operations, requiring DTIA to assess whether data transfers support business continuity without violating sanctions.

requirements.text: The Minister of Finance may permit financial services for business necessities
The Minister of Finance may permit dealing in affected property for business necessities
Permitted activities include interest accruals and other earnings
Permitted activities include contractual payments that predate sanctions

sections.source_quote: The Minister of Finance may also permit the provision of financial services or the dealing in affected property which are not related to providing for basic living expenses, but which are necessary in the normal course of business
sections.relevance_reason: Financial exceptions for sanctioned persons

sections.heading: The permission of the Minister of Finance is granted by means of written
sections.title: Formal permission process for sanctioned persons
sections.summary: The Minister of Finance grants permission for sanctioned persons through written communication. The Financial Intelligence Centre must publish these permissions on its website to ensure transparency and awareness among institutions and stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires formal documentation and public disclosure of sanctioned person permissions, directly impacting DTIA by mandating transparency in cross-border data transfers involving sanctioned entities.

requirements.text: The permission of the Minister of Finance is granted via written communication
The Director of the Centre must notify accountable institutions of permissions
The Centre publishes permissions on its website with conditions
